name: Sardine Run
shortDescription: Annual northward migration of billions of sardines along South Africa's Wild Coast, attracting common dolphins, bronze whalers, dusky sharks, Bryde's whales, and Cape gannets into spectacular bait-ball feeding events.
requiredExperience: Comfortable in cold open water, surface conditions, and rapid boat entries. Open Water minimum; Advanced strongly recommended. Most operators run snorkel-and-scuba hybrid trips.
ethicsNotes: Maintain distance from cetaceans; do not chase or block bait balls. Follow operator briefings on minimum distance from whales.
conservationNotes: Sardine populations are climate-sensitive; recent runs have been smaller and farther offshore. Choose operators that contribute sightings data to research programmes.
limitations: Sightings vary year to year. Some seasons produce no full-day bait balls. Confidence is based on multi-year occurrence clustering, not a per-trip probability.

locations.notes: Operators stage daily out of Port St Johns / Coffee Bay during peak weeks.

name: Great White Cage Diving
shortDescription: Surface-cage encounters with white sharks at established aggregation sites. Non-decompression, no certification required at most operators.
requiredExperience: No certification required for surface cage. Free-diving extensions require breath-hold competence.
ethicsNotes: Only book operators with permitted, regulated chumming practices. Avoid operators that bait sharks toward divers outside cages.
conservationNotes: White sharks are listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. Regional populations have shifted (notably away from Gansbaai toward Mossel Bay) and operator availability changes year to year.
limitations: Sighting frequency is operator-reported. We do not present a per-trip probability without a documented effort denominator.

locations.notes: Closest current scubaSeason location to active white shark cage operators on the South African coast.

name: Scalloped Hammerhead Schools
shortDescription: Daytime schooling aggregations of scalloped hammerheads at seamounts and current-swept island walls.
requiredExperience: Advanced Open Water minimum, comfort with strong current, blue-water hangs, and deep multilevel profiles. Reef-hook training recommended.
ethicsNotes: Stay grouped, breathe shallowly, no flash photography at the school. Operator briefings supersede general guidance.
conservationNotes: Scalloped hammerheads are Critically Endangered (IUCN). Aggregations are sensitive to repeated disturbance.
limitations: Schooling behavior varies with current, lunar phase, and water temperature. Encounter frequency is reported by liveaboard operators rather than measured against a survey denominator.

name: Whale Shark Aggregations
shortDescription: Predictable seasonal aggregations of the world's largest fish, typically at plankton or fish-spawning hotspots.
requiredExperience: Most encounters are surface snorkel or shallow scuba. Open Water minimum where scuba is permitted; many regulated sites are snorkel-only.
ethicsNotes: Maintain minimum approach distance (typically 3 m head, 4 m tail), no touching, no flash. Follow national whale-shark codes of conduct.
conservationNotes: Endangered on the IUCN Red List. Choose permitted operators in regulated sites; avoid feeding-tour operations where they are controversial.
limitations: Aggregation timing varies year to year with prey availability. We report occurrence and operator seasonality, not per-trip probability.

name: Manta Ray Cleaning Stations
shortDescription: Reliable cleaning-station encounters where mantas hover for wrasse and butterflyfish cleaners.
requiredExperience: Open Water; current and reef-hook experience helpful at Pacific channel sites.
ethicsNotes: Stay below or beside the cleaning station, never above the mantas. No touching, no chasing.
conservationNotes: Reef manta is Vulnerable, oceanic manta is Endangered (IUCN). Support sites with no-anchor and no-touch policies.
limitations: Visit rate varies with current and time of day; cleaning behavior is highly site-specific.

locations.notes: Night manta dives target plankton-light aggregations, not classical cleaning stations.

name: Thresher Shark Cleaning
shortDescription: Dawn dives on Monad Shoal where pelagic threshers ascend from deep water to be cleaned by wrasses.
requiredExperience: Advanced Open Water; comfortable with pre-dawn entry, 25-30 m depth, and motionless hangs behind the rope line.
ethicsNotes: Stay behind the designated rope; no flash; do not approach the threshers in open water.
conservationNotes: Pelagic thresher is Endangered (IUCN). Monad Shoal is a Marine Protected Area; respect dive-count and depth limits.
limitations: Sightings are operator-tracked and seasonal swell can close the site. We do not publish per-dive probability.

name: Mobula Ray Aggregations
shortDescription: Vast schools of Munk's devil rays moving through the Sea of Cortez, with leaping displays at the surface.
requiredExperience: Open Water; comfort with freediving / snorkel encounters and rapid in-out boat work.
ethicsNotes: Do not swim through schools; let rays pass. No flash photography close to schools.
conservationNotes: Mobulids are bycatch-vulnerable; choose operators that participate in mobulid research.
limitations: Timing shifts with water temperature and prey distribution. Confidence is medium; encounter is occurrence-clustered, not survey-measured.

locations.notes: Mobula encounters supplement the manta/dolphin/shark season here.

name: Blackwater Diving
shortDescription: Open-ocean night dives over deep water, tethered to a lighted drift line, observing larval and pelagic invertebrates ascending in the diel migration.
requiredExperience: Advanced Open Water + Night specialty strongly recommended. Comfort with neutral buoyancy in featureless water, tether discipline, and small-subject photography.
ethicsNotes: Do not collect specimens. Avoid touching gelatinous animals (siphonophores can sting through skin).
conservationNotes: Diel migration is a foundational ocean carbon pathway; minimize light pollution outside the dive window.
limitations: Subject diversity is highly variable and weather-dependent. We do not list specific species probabilities.

locations.notes: One of the longest-running commercial blackwater operations.

name: Mass Coral Spawning
shortDescription: Synchronized broadcast spawning of stony corals, typically a few nights after a full moon in late spring or summer.
requiredExperience: Open Water + Night specialty. Comfort with low-vis night reef diving and minimal-light protocols.
ethicsNotes: Reduce dive-light intensity during gamete release. No touching or finning above coral heads.
conservationNotes: Spawning timing is increasingly affected by ocean temperature anomalies; report observed spawn timing to local reef-monitoring programmes.
limitations: Exact night varies year to year and locally. Predictions are operator-tracked, not published as probabilities.

locations.notes: GBR spawning typically a few nights after the November or early-December full moon.

name: Mandarin Fish Dusk Spawning
shortDescription: Daily dusk mating ascents of mandarin fish above rubble patches, observable on a single short shallow dive.
requiredExperience: Open Water; excellent buoyancy and patience matter more than depth experience.
ethicsNotes: Minimal dive lights, no strobes during the ascent itself. Do not crowd known spawning rubble.
conservationNotes: Repeated bright-light exposure can disrupt the ascent. Follow operator etiquette strictly.
limitations: Ascent is brief (often only minutes). Daily presence is consistent at established sites but not a guarantee.

name: Giant Australian Cuttlefish Aggregation
shortDescription: Annual breeding aggregation of giant cuttlefish in the shallow waters of upper Spencer Gulf, with elaborate male display and mating behavior.
requiredExperience: Open Water; shore-entry, cold-water, and dry/thick-suit comfort. Many divers do this as snorkel.
ethicsNotes: Do not handle cuttlefish; avoid contact with eggs on the reef underside.
conservationNotes: The Spencer Gulf aggregation crashed in the 2010s and has partially recovered; treat the site as ecologically fragile.
limitations: Numbers fluctuate; not a precise per-dive probability.
